data.title: Power Integrations: Efficiency Is The Ultimate Clean Energy
data.description:
Power Integrations (POWI) is a unique company that we have been following for some time. We started with a "Sell" rating as we thought the company's valuation was excessive, and since then, shares have lost roughly half their value. However, we probably became optimistic too soon, seeing shares as attractively priced late last year, but shares have underperformed the market since then. Still, the underperformance against the iShares Global Clean Energy ETF (ICLN), and in particular the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), has been less dramatic.

As a reminder, Power Integrations is a small company with big ambitions. It is widely considered the global leader in Gallium Nitride (GaN) high-voltage power conversion. This technology can be significantly more efficient compared to silicon, and in terms of cost it is approaching cost parity. For this reason, it is believed that GaN technology is the future of high-voltage power conversion, and Power Integrations controls some of the key IP.

High-voltage is critical if you want fast-charging, which is why many mobile phone chargers now use integrated circuits from Power Integrations. While we do not know if BYD (BYDDF) is using GaN technology to charge its EVs in five minutes, it was shared that the charging power was 1,000 kilowatts. This likely means using very high voltage.

Power Integrations can tailor their technology to make purpose-built solutions for a wide range of applications. These include smartphone chargers, LEDs, electric locomotives, motor drives, and electric vehicles. Power Integrations introduced 900 V and 1250 V products in 2023, and 1700 V in 2024. The higher voltages should unlock new applications and more impressive performance.

Attractive Industry

Power Integrations is a niche player in an already attractive industry of analogue chips. One reason the industry is attractive is the high switching costs customers would incur if they decide to replace one of Power Integrations’ chips from their designs. They would have to spend considerable time and engineering resources re-designing and re-validating the products, and adds risks that the new version might include error triggering product recalls. This means that once one of Power Integrations’ chips is added to a product design it tends to stay in the bill of materials. One statistic the company shared to back this up is that 52% of its 2024 sales came from product families introduced before 2002.

Financials

Power Integrations has a good track record of revenue and earnings growth, but there was an enormous demand pull-forward during the COVID pandemic when people decided to splurge on new appliances, electronics, and other consumer goods. The normalization has been painful for the company, but there were recent signs that the company was returning to growth. Of course, this was before tariff disruptions, so it remains to be seen if the nascent recovery is short-circuited by recent developments.

Taking a longer-term view, Power Integrations has a good track record of growing revenue at a faster pace compared to the analogue chip industry, with a CAGR roughly 2% higher. Its 2024 revenue was similar to that of 2019 given the demand pull-forward, but we do expect growth to return at some point as the company launches new innovative and more capable chips, and as many of its end-markets are growing rapidly.

Shareholder Returns

Power Integrations pays a small dividend, but it has been increasing and at current prices yields almost 2%. The company also increased the dividend by 5% at the end of last year. Shares outstanding have declined, down close to 13% since 2008, as the company has repurchased shares opportunistically at times when it has considered they were trading at attractive valuations.

For example, in 2022 it spent roughly twice its free cash flow in share repurchases, significantly more than what it spent on the dividend or previous share buybacks.

In November 2024, Power Integrations announced a new $50 million repurchase authorization. It is one of the companies we have seen take more decisive action with respect to repurchases when valuations appear attractive, and it tends to significantly reduce buybacks once the share price recovers.

Balance Sheet

One reason Power Integrations has been able to react so strongly with significant buybacks when the share price has decreased is the flexibility provided by its solid balance sheet. The company has basically no long-term debt, and ended 2024 with roughly $300 million in cash and short-term investments.

Short-term Headwinds

There are good reasons why the share price has been under pressure for the last couple of years. As already mentioned, part of it was the resulting weakness after demand was pull-forward during the pandemic boom for consumer appliances. There were signs the company was starting to grow revenue once again, although that will be put to the test as the result of the trade war.

Another temporary headwind is that current market conditions have resulted in the Japanese Yen (FXY) appreciating given its haven currency characteristics. Power Integrations benefits from a weaker Yen, as it lowers its manufacturing costs, resulting in higher gross margins. In addition, there is uncertainty as to the potential cost impact that tariffs on Japan could have on production costs.

Long-term Tailwinds

While the short to medium term looks complicated for the company, especially if the announced tariffs remain in place for a prolonged period of time, we remain optimistic about the long-term prospects for the company.

For example, its serviceable addressable market [SAM] is expected to double by 2027 compared to where it was in 2022. The company is also further diversifying into new end-markets, many of which have strong megatrends in their favor. For example, there is an important opportunity for the company with electric vehicles, an end-market expected to grow around the world, but also an opportunity where the company believes it can increase dollar content per unit from tens of dollars to over $100 per vehicle. Other attractive end-markets include micromobility applications like e-bikes and e-scooters, smart appliances, and building automation.

Perhaps one of the biggest specific opportunities lies in helping power data centers, which have on average 2.5 power supplies per server. Given the increased power needs for AI workloads this opens new opportunities for GaN solutions.

Future Outlook

While the short term to medium term continues to look complicated for the company, we continue to believe this company has attractive long-term prospects. It might require patience, especially if the U.S. economy enters recession or if the tariff war continues to escalate. Still, we believe the important high-voltage semiconductor niche is being underappreciated by investors. High-voltage solutions will be critical for increased energy efficiency, and to enable many technologies required for a lower carbon future.

The cleanest energy is the one that is not wasted, which is why efficient energy consumption and transmission is so important. At the same time, as GaN technology continues to improve, with higher voltage ICs and lower cost, we expect new applications to be unlocked and for the potential market to continue expanding.

Valuation

At first look, Power Integrations appears expensive, but there are two important factors to remember. One is that profitability has been significantly pressured by lower revenues as things normalize after the Covid demand pull-forward.

Compared to historical peak earnings, current prices would translate to a ~15x Price/Earnings multiple. Another factor to consider is that the company has a balance sheet with a net cash position amounting to ~$5 per share.

In fact, compared to other analogue chip peers, Power Integrations appears attractively priced on a Price/Sales ratio. The company currently has a similar forward Price/Earnings multiple to Texas Instruments (TXN), but still trades at a premium compared to Analog Devices (ADI). Still, we believe Power Integrations deserves a premium valuation given the attractiveness of the GaN power semiconductor chips niche.

Risks

The biggest risk we currently see is a potential recession in the U.S., which would significantly reduce demand for appliances and consumer products. The other major risk is the potential production cost impact if tariffs on Japan are maintained at current levels. These risks are mitigated by a strong balance sheet, and significant end-market diversification. Roughly 35% of revenue comes from the industrial and auto segment, 39% from consumer devices, 14% from computers, and 12% from communications which is mostly mobile phone chargers.

Conclusion

Power Integrations is an under-followed small-cap company operating in the already attractive industry of analogue chips, and specialized in the promising niche of GaN power devices. While the company is facing short-term and medium term headwinds, its long-term prospects remain attractive with many secular trends in its favor. There are important risks to consider, but these are mitigated by end-market diversification, high customer switching costs, and a very strong balance sheet. Using trailing-twelve-month valuation metrics, shares appear expensive, but on a forward basis and adjusting for the net cash per share, they look significantly more attractively priced.

data.title: Artisan Developing World Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Dear Fellow Shareholder:

Market Backdrop

The Artisan Developing World Fund (MUTF:ARTYX, Investor Class) returned 1.98% for the quarter ended March 31, 2025, versus 2.93% for the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (all returns in USD unless stated otherwise). Since June 30, 2015, the Artisan Developing World Fund has returned 155.27% cumulatively, versus 42.90% for the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. The quarter began with perceived US economic momentum and notions of US exceptionalism. Markets were then uprooted by China’s artificial intelligence achievements, global tariff uncertainty, and an end to the so-called German debt brake. Chinese markets were standout performers (MSCI China Index +15.02%) against a backdrop of tariffs and geopolitical tensions, as a Chinese hedge fund released an efficient AI reasoning model (DEEPSEEK) that underscored the appeal of China’s technology capabilities. This model achievement also led investors to question the prodigious sums being spent on AI capital formation in the US. As a result, the S&P 500® Index (SP500, SPX) declined 4.27% during the quarter, while the tech-heavy Taiwanese market declined 12.63% (MSCI Taiwan Index). Exacerbating this dynamic, Germany responded to uncertainty over the US commitment to Ukraine by releasing the debt brake to invest in both military capabilities and depleted domestic infrastructure. The MSCI ACWI ex USA Index rose 5.23% as investors flocked to European banks and other perceived beneficiaries of European fiscal largesse.

Currencies also reflected an increased preference for non-US assets (MSCI EM Currency Index +1.74%, euro +4.32%, yen +5.10%). From a domestic EM standpoint, Brazil rose (MSCI Brazil Index +14.05%) despite increasingly tight monetary conditions, perhaps as investors saw levered plays on non-US exposure and Brazil made progress on a Mercosur-European trade deal. Central and Eastern European markets rose as investors similarly sought disproportionate beneficiaries of European fiscal dynamics (MSCI Poland Index +31.27%, MSCI Czech Republic Index +28.66%). South Africa rose (MSCI South Africa Index +13.84%), likely along similar lines. Weaker markets included Indonesia, as President Prabowo’s signature free meal policy created discomfort about the country’s fiscal trajectory and productivity outlook. Turkey also declined (MSCI Turkey Index -8.96%) as the government arrested Istanbul’s mayor, who is a key opposition figure.

Contributors and Detractors

Top contributors to performance for the quarter included Southeast Asian e-commerce and gaming platform Sea (SE), Latin American online platform company MercadoLibre (MELI), Chinese online recruitment services company Kanzhun (BZ), Chinese Internet and technology company Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY), and electronic payments network Visa (V). Sea benefited from accelerating growth and monetization in e-commerce, renewed promise in its gaming business, and strong on and off-platform development in financial services.

MercadoLibre experienced sustained momentum in its e-commerce business due to gross merchandise value (GMV) growth and take rate realization, and good fintech development as credit expansion continued while provisions moderated. Kanzhun benefited from improving recruitment demand and optimism around the impact of AI on both job postings and its own operations. Tencent benefited from renewed investor confidence in Chinese technology companies following the release of DeepSeek, especially given Tencent’s strong communications ecosystem, cloud capabilities, and distribution advantages. Visa benefited from reaccelerating volume growth and stronger-than-expected cross- border performance, and perhaps from elevated inflation and nominal spending.

Bottom contributors to performance for the quarter included Indian food delivery and quick commerce company Zomato, Australian logistics services software company WiseTech Global (OTCPK:WTCHF), graphics semiconductor company Nvidia (NVDA), Indian online travel company MakeMyTrip (MMYT), and semiconductor design and simulation company Cadence (CDNS). Zomato declined amid weaker consumer sentiment in India and concerns about the increasingly competitive environment in its “Quick Commerce” segment, against the backdrop of continued capital-raising activity from competitors.

WiseTech fell due to governance concerns after the resignation of independent board directors related to disputes with the company’s founder, and delays in new product introductions.

Nvidia fell as DeepSeek’s strong efficiency gains catalyzed fears of industry oversupply, and Nvidia became a proxy for US exceptionalism. MakeMyTrip fell due to concerns about the slowdown of consumer sentiment in India, despite continued resilience in domestic and outbound travel. Cadence declined due to signs of sustained industry pressure in its core digital and analog semiconductor design business and continued cautious commentary around its China business due to geopolitical constraints.

Market Outlook

Policy uncertainty continues to permeate markets. The dollar (USDOLLAR,DXY) has recently weakened against most currencies. Investors have been left to contemplate the possibility that US exceptionalism has peaked, and that industrial policy could weaken the dollar and US markets further. At the same time, it is notable that the MSCI Emerging Markets ex China Index declined 1.72% during this quarter. The reality is that the US is an engine for growth and key trading partner for many emerging markets countries. To the extent that the US lowers interest rates to support economic growth, real rates suggest only limited scope for interest rate reductions in many emerging countries. Moreover, we have long emphasized that lower rates are not a panacea for economic development in most emerging countries, which suffer from a lack of domestic capital formation and foreign investment. In fact, regardless of the price of money (i.e., the level of interest rates), the US’ increased focus on domestic priorities and Europe’s pivot toward defense could mean that capital formation in emerging markets is even more elusive in the coming years. To the extent that capital formation is the key constraint on investment-led growth, job creation, and real income growth in emerging markets, broader economic participation in these countries may remain elusive. China does have some potential to fill this gap in emerging markets as an exporter of capital and technology, but its domestic priorities may take precedent, especially over shorter periods. More realistically, China may provide emerging markets with compelling products at competitive price points.

As for China, we have written extensively about China’s ecosystems for domestic capital formation, which are unique in an emerging markets context and perhaps only eclipsed globally by those of the United States. The DeepSeek achievement reflects the necessity of domestic technology innovation in China given Western export restrictions, and the engineering talent and capital resources in the country. Indeed, Chinese electronic vehicle pioneer BYD’s (OTCPK:BYDDF) recent achievements in fast EV charging and autonomous driving systems (which will be embedded for free in its cars) also reflect unique ecosystems for domestic capital formation. These ecosystems have the potential to flourish, even in the absence of access to Western technology. At the same time, China’s capital formation capability while palpable for key industrial priorities has largely been dormant the last few years, which is a key reason why the economy has decelerated so markedly. Thus, a key question surrounds the extent to which private capital formation accelerates in China, which would reduce the need for China to provide fiscal support and bolster property markets. While many companies in China have spoken recently of AI-driven strategies to increase revenues and reduce costs, the reality is that AI development is for now a more tangible concept than improved economic development. We have been careful to create and maintain pathways for reflation in China assets over the last several years while limiting our capital at risk if, for example, tensions with the US were to escalate.

Portfolio Positioning

We developed the concept of “Flexion” out of necessity, at a moment of crisis. It was a tool for us to add to positions in a contrarian way, while simultaneously allowing for a pathway to deemphasize that investment if necessary. In more recent iterations, we have defined Flexion in large part as follows: adding to positions with progression to a lower weight and trimming with progression to a higher weight. Practiced with patience and consistency, Flexion can become a tool for an asset allocation outcome. For example, over the last several years, we took capital back from Nvidia with progression to a higher or maximum weight. In the aggregate, we estimate that we have taken back approximately 1,800bps (net of buys) from our Nvidia investment since establishing our position in the first half of 2019. In recent months, as the US AI capital spending boom has been called into question, Nvidia has started to naturally underperform our portfolio. Exiting the quarter, it represented just 2.8% of our investments. While we have not yet endeavored to add to Nvidia with progression to a lower weight, the point is that this process has been occurring naturally, throughout our portfolio. Thus, while we do not feel qualified to call for an end to US exceptionalism (nor would we have predicted it to begin with), our asset allocation over a period of time has begun to reflect precisely this possibility— even as we add selectively to US Passporters at the margin.

Notably, of our top 10 holdings, Visa (which we own primarily for correlation benefits) and CrowdStrike (CRWD) are the only US-based Passporters. Three years ago, there were 6 US-based Passporters in our top 10. Similarly, we are positioned to allow for some progression to a higher weight in our China holdings, while taking capital back if this occurs. Ultimately, our positioning for an end to US exceptionalism, or alternatively a continuation of it, is to a large degree an outcome of movements within the portfolio. If an investment or country allocation deteriorates beyond our expectations, we have established a pathway to deemphasize that investment or country.

Last quarter, we articulated an emphasis on EM assets exposed to large population clusters with access to credit and formal employment that can benefit disproportionately from consumption. It is this alignment that creates the potential for revenue velocity despite economic limitations, which when paired with attractive incremental margin structures can create the potential for disproportionate equity outcomes. For example, Sea has been a successful investment not because of rapid growth in the middle class, but rather because young employed tech-savvy people have capable smartphones that they are using to buy basic goods and in many cases access credit for the first time outside the banking system. MakeMyTrip has been conducive to disproportionate equity outcomes in part because formally employed workers are aspiring to domestic travel, and because large and wealthy niche populations in India are increasingly traveling abroad. By contrast, a narrow focus on domicile can implicitly hold investors captive to a production-driven construct (i.e., capital formation in the emerging markets with limited potential for convergence). By contrast, consumption growth tied to the modern economy and attractive population clusters can significantly exceed the pace of EM capital formation. Notably, three of our top four performers since inception are domiciled in the emerging markets, and approximately two thirds of our performance since inception has come from EM-domiciled companies. In other words, our willingness to look beyond production-driven constraints embedded in the domicile construct has been a key determinant of the success of our EM-domiciled investments. Moreover, our Passport company exposure provides additional conduits to Western ecosystems for capital formation, aspirational products and foundational technologies. These products and services, in turn, hold appeal to the aforementioned population clusters that permeate the emerging world.

We thank you for your trust and confidence.

Lewis S. Kaufman, CFA

Investment Process

We seek to build, preserve and enhance a stream of compounded business value. We define this emphasis as follows:

Build: Pair low penetration domestic demand with scalable and enduring businesses that are able to drive value creation and disproportionate outcomes.

Preserve: Preserve value creation and establish a forward-looking construct for managing risk.

Enhance: Leverage value pathways to enhance long-term value creation.

data.title: Vertiv: Still A Strong Buy As Sell-Off Looks Overdone
data.description:
I last covered Vertiv Holdings (VRT) about two months back in February after the company reported its full-year 2024 results, with another strong quarter delivering 25.7% year-on-year growth across its topline and a notable 380 bps improvement in its adjusted operating margins. The company’s strong results, along with its compelling valuation post the significant drop in the VRT’s stock price, largely due to sell-off in tech stocks triggered by the DeepSeek's announcement, prompted me to give VRT a rating upgrade to “Strong Buy” as the company’s fundamentals remain intact.

However, the sell-off didn’t end there and continued further due to broader market sentiments turning negative over tariff fears and concerns around a slowdown in orders for data center equipment from mega-caps like Microsoft and Google. The prolonged sell-off driven by these factors collectively wiped out VRT’s stock price gain of nearly 100% over the past year within a few months in 2025 and is currently down in double-digit percentage since my last VRT article. In this follow-up article, I will discuss VRT's trajectory after the massive drop in the stock price and unpack how the new tariffs' developments shape VRT’s outlook moving forward.

Major Supports for Vertiv's Future Growth

In my last article, I highlighted the company’s continued strong performance across the regions as order growth remains robust due to a healthy demand environment, particularly from colocation and hyperscalers. This robust order growth resulted in a backlog of over $7.2 billion as of Q4 2024, which still continues to provide strong visibility for topline growth in the quarters ahead, even if there is a near-term demand slowdown for data center-related equipment triggered by macro noise.

In addition to this strong backlog cushioning, I believe that the need for physical infrastructure development will remain crucial despite the fears from the hardware-light AI like DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) R1, due to the energy needs and density constraints. In my view, the investments in AI infrastructure are still in an early stage. Considering this, VRT’s positioning in the high-density data center cooling and power should continue to support the demand for the company’s equipment and solutions in the future as density and energy needs continue to rise, making VRT a mission-critical player and not optional.

As VRT also continues to focus on expanding its manufacturing capacity to benefit from the infrastructure cycles, I believe this should further help the company gain market share in this consolidation-prone industry in the longer term, driving topline growth in the coming years.

How Vertiv Stands To Benefit From Tariffs?

The recent tariff developments and challenging macroeconomic environment have been painful for the economies globally, and not just for the United States, leading to a bloodbath on Wall Street. As a result, the S&P 500, the benchmark index, has tumbled nearly 13% YTD. Talking about VRT, it has been among the worst stocks so far in 2025, dropping nearly 60% from its highs in January.

While the fears around tariffs and negative broader market sentiment have negatively impacted the investor's confidence in VRT’s future growth prospects, I believe the recent tariff development, especially on Chinese tech and critical components, provides significant tailwinds for Vertiv in the long term.

In my last article, we discussed VRT's focus on expanding its local manufacturing footprint across the United States to strengthen its supply chain to address potential tariff risk. Under this focus, VRT has significantly invested in capacity expansion, which, I believe, should ensure uninterrupted operations, helping the company deal with anticipated volume growth in the quarters ahead, driven by continued AI-related investments across the region.

Tariffs imposed on Chinese companies should also accelerate market share gains for Vertiv, especially in price-sensitive markets, where Chinese players were previously stronger due to the cost advantage from low-cost exports. This should also benefit VRT’s pricing power in the region, potentially pushing its margins upwards. Overall, I think, apart from VRT’s strong market position due to its products and solutions, the buy American trend, reinforced by the tariffs, stands to benefit VRT in the coming years, with the U.S. federal and enterprise customers increasingly prioritizing leading domestic suppliers, which should support the company’s business and help it deliver above market growth in the coming years.

Valuation Update And Final Verdict

Since my last VRT coverage, the company’s stock has been down nearly 40% as it continues to face the sell-off that started in January this year after the Deep Seek shock. However, this sell-off has significantly cooled down the stock valuation, which was trading near 40x its earnings earlier this year when the stock price hit a high of $155.8.

Currently, the company's stock is trading at a non-GAAP forward P/E ratio of 17.77, based on its FY25 EPS estimates of $3.56. This is a significant improvement from what it was a few months ago, and also represents a notable discount in comparison to its historical average multiple of 24.93x, considering its strong double-digit forward growth, significantly above its sector average.

Going ahead, I expect the continued healthy demand environment for the company's products and solutions should support order growth across all the regions in the quarters ahead. This, along with support from the elevated backlog, should continue to support the company in delivering above-market growth in 2025 despite noise around a slowdown in demand for data center-related equipment.

As discussed in my previous article, the company remains focused on excellent project execution and improving operational efficiency to drive margin growth. I believe, in addition to these factors, VRT should also gain some pricing power from the cost disadvantages to foreign players from tariffs, further benefiting the company's overall margins and bottom line in the future.

Overall, while the macroeconomic challenges persist, the company appears to be strongly positioned to continue its outperformance in the quarters ahead. I believe that the tariff-related risks are already de-risked for Vertiv, as we discussed earlier in the article. I think it might even accelerate its long-term growth trajectory. Therefore, considering these factors, the recent drop in the stock price presents an excellent opportunity to invest in this strong AI player. Hence, I am still strongly bullish on the company's growth prospects and would like to maintain my "Strong Buy" rating on the stock at the current levels for investors with a long-term view.

data.title: First Solar: Still A Buy, But Watching The Margins
data.description:
First Solar was the very first stock I covered as a Seeking Alpha analyst. Unfortunately, I can’t say my call was right at the time, with the stock underperforming the S&P by almost 20%.

My thesis was based on a simple idea: even with the Trump administration, the IRA credits wouldn't be revoked, and the stock looked cheap enough. I was actually right on the credits—but also a bit naive to think the market wouldn’t react to the heightened risk perception by punishing the stock.

In this article, I’ll go through what happened in the meantime and look at past earnings and the new perspectives now that the impact of the tariffs is clearer.

Earnings Calls

Q32024

Third quarter earnings call was still a solid print, with 27% YoY revenue growth and 50% gross margins. EPS came in at $2.91, way above expectations of $2.09. The key driver for the beat was still the favorable position the company had in the US due to IRA incentives. However, management revised its volume guidance down, citing operational challenges that caused delays in manufacturing (hurricanes, strikes, security alerts, etc).

There were some issues worth mentioning besides the delays, which seemed non-recurring:

  • Continued disruption in the India solar market: India was seeing a wave of low-cost Chinese panels entering the market and disrupting FSLR’s product. By that time, management decided to redirect some capacity from India to export to the US—and that’s important because that solution won’t be as easy now due to the tariffs.
  • Quality issues with Series 7: They recognized a $50M warranty charge in the quarter due to a manufacturing error and an error in the tested engineering performance margin. Both problems have been remediated.

Q42024

Fourth quarter wasn’t good for the thesis. While revenue came in at record levels with 30% Y/Y growth and record sales of 14.1GW, the earnings miss was sharp: $3.65 vs. $4.73. Key points of the miss were:

  • Warranty charges (again!): They booked another $6M related to manufacturing issues with Series 7.
  • Ramp-related charges: Between the Alabama ramp-up and the CuRe conversion in Ohio, they absorbed another $22M in underutilization and start-up costs.
  • Logistics: Continued manufacturing issues caused delays and rescheduling, which resulted in higher warehousing and transport costs—totaling $36M+.
  • Tax credit haircut: The company sold $857M in 45X tax credits, taking a $39M cut to do so, impacting gross margin by 3% in Q4 alone.

I think that at this point the market is right to start asking questions. In Q3, the problems seemed temporary, but as they persist, we’re seeing less-than-optimal execution from the company, to say the least.

Plus, they are acknowledging that the new geopolitical context is reducing production flexibility a lot, as they can’t really relocate capacity to export like they used to—because markets are closing for imports:

While we enter 2025 in a strong position with respect to our US production, we are in an under-allocation position for our Series 6 Malaysia and Vietnam production. This is driven in large part by two factors: one, customers employing module delivery shift rights; and the other, the previously mentioned contract terminations that occurred in 2024.

While typically, we would seek to mitigate the impact of these factors by reallocating shifted or terminated deliveries to other customers, our ability to do so in the near term—particularly as it relates to our Southeast Asia-produced product—is constrained by the policy environment in Europe, India, and the US.

First Solar's ability to reallocate excess (Series 6) production from Malaysia and Vietnam has been sharply reduced. India has effectively blocked all module imports from those regions, imposing restrictive trade policies that prioritize domestic content. Meanwhile, the U.S. has ramped up trade barriers slapping tariffs as high as 46% on Vietnam imports and 25% on Malaysian ones.

Tariffs and Uncertainty

New American tariffs have a double effect. On one side, they reduce the flexibility of repurposing installed capacity at international sites (which leads to factory underutilization and gross margin penalties, as we saw in Q4) and also raise product costs, since even the cadmium telluride used in the thin film is imported.

First Solar might be forced to take more definitive steps to further reduce capacity or even shut down international factories in order to protect margins—especially considering that commoditized products suffer heavily from underutilization.

They previously said:

We continue to see long-term opportunities to place international products in the US and optimize our allocation position.

Well—not anymore.

From a cost structure standpoint, however, they seem to be hedged:

As of December 31, 2024, over 90% of our backlog had some form of steel and/or aluminum commodity cost protection, and substantially all of our backlog had some form of freight protection.

It's also good to know that 70% of Indian production is also destined for the American market. At least they're seeing tariffs of 26%, smaller than Chinese ones. They also talked about that:

Note, our module sale contracts for international product deliveries typically have some form of tariff protection if new tariffs are imposed on the importation of modules into the US, whether in the form of First Solar termination right or tariff absorption that is either shared with the customer or exclusively borne by the customer.

Another possible (huge) risk is Chinese countermeasures, like blocking the exports of minerals used in the manufacturing process:

(...) export controls concerning five critical minerals, including products containing tellurium announced by China's Ministry of Commerce earlier this month. Although tellurium and products containing tellurium, including among them cadmium telluride, are key raw materials used in our module production process, we have over the past decade employed a strategic sourcing strategy to diversify our tellurium supply chain to mitigate a sole sourcing position in China and are undertaking additional measures to mitigate dependencies on China for certain products containing tellurium.

On the other side, tariffs consolidate FSLR’s position in the domestic utility market, as Chinese panels will face a significant price increase. It also adds to the uncertainty of relying on Chinese partners for utility companies, especially with the risk of trade war escalation. FSLR becomes a safer choice.

Besides, there’s always the possibility of the 45X credits being revoked under the new administration, which adds another layer of uncertainty regarding the company’s future earnings.

With all these risks and considerations, management is guiding for EPS in the range of $17-$20, from revenue between $5.3 and $5.8 billion, and gross margins at 47%.

For now, the market seems to be buying it:

Is it Cheap Enough?

In terms of multiples FSLR is at its lowest levels.

However, earnings in sellers of commoditized products are volatile, and there are a lot of risks to consider in this evaluation.

The first and foremost is the IRA revision. Let’s take a look at the 2025 guidance to get a sense of that:

Gross profit, on average, is expected to be $2.6 billion, with IRA credits accounting for around $1.7 billion. So, 65% of gross profit is tied to those credits. Without them, gross margins would drop to somewhere near 16% instead of the guided 47%, and EPS would fall to $2.75 instead of the guided $28.35 on average. So yes, that’s a huge dependency. First Solar’s valuation today largely hinges on the continuation of the IRA credits.

The repeal of those credits seems unlikely, given the legislative hurdles and the fact that Trump is ultimately aiming to bring production back onshore. I do think it doesn’t make much sense to revoke them. But then again, I also didn’t think massive tariffs made sense either — and here we are.

The other risks are more intrinsic to the business operation.

Guidance includes $55 million of ramp-on underutilization and $55 million in startup costs. However, it’s still not clear where they will allocate the approximately 1 GW of international volume.

Based on the guided numbers, First Solar is expected to generate $5.55B in revenue with an ASP of $0.29/W and a cost of $0.20/W. That implies shipments of around 19.1 GW and total costs of $3.83B.

If 1 GW isn’t sold, total output drops to 18.1 GW, but total fixed costs stay the same (assuming all cost is fixed as a proxy) — meaning cost per watt rises from $0.20 to $0.211. That’s the underutilization effect: a $200M gross profit hit.

This would impact both revenue (–$290M) and gross profit (–$200M). All else equal, I estimate that earnings could fall ~10% from the average EPS guidance. That’s something the multiple likely already accounts for.

I also believe that ramp-up underutilization and startup cost assumptions for the year aren’t particularly conservative — especially if we take the last two quarters as a reference.

Also, given the escalation of tariffs, I’m quite sure that even with hedging, the contracts won’t fully protect the company. As discussed, 50% of their capacity is in the U.S. This cost increase will likely impact earnings as well. Considering the total COGS for the 51% of capacity in the U.S., a 50% hedge ratio, and ~30% tariffs, this would lead to somewhere near $200M in additional cost — and that’s a very conservative estimate, assuming all costs are variable and imported. $100M is probably a more reasonable figure for the increase in imported variable costs.

All in all, these two factors could impact gross profit in $300M and earnings by ~15% combined (from average levels).

Valuation and verdict

First Solar’s valuation already reflects a good portion of the earnings headwinds, as we can see below.

While I do see short-term risks to earnings, I also think tariffs help consolidate the company’s position in the U.S., which could allow for pricing power in the longer term.

I'm a fundamental analyst, and based on what I see, the company is still a buy. However, we’re in a volatile market — especially for businesses that sell physical goods — and earnings will likely face further downward revisions next quarter. I’d consider building a position very gradually, targeting a long-term portfolio weight, rather than rushing in or waiting for the next earnings to make a move.

The biggest risk to this thesis remains the IRA credits. If they’re taken away, the impact would be immediate and clearly disruptive to the investment case.

data.title: ClearBridge Mid Cap Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
By Brian Angerame & Matthew Lilling, CFA

Mid Caps Show Relative Strength in Down Market

Market Overview

Despite an optimistic start to the year, the threat of tariffs and trade policy changes under the new Trump administration and a rotation out of AI-related tech stocks spurred volatility and uncertainty that sent equities lower for the first quarter. Amid the decline, mid caps broadly outperformed other areas of the market with the Russell Midcap Index declining -3.40% compared to the -4.27%, -4.49% and -4.72% declines of the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX), the Russell 1000 Index and the Russell 3000 Index, respectively. Concerns over the economic ramifications of new tariffs, declining consumer sentiment and obfuscation of the trajectory of interest rates and earnings helped value stocks seize leadership from growth stocks with the Russell Midcap Value Index returning -2.11%, 500 basis points ahead of the Russell Midcap Growth Index.

From a sector standpoint, energy (+7.25%) was the top performer within the benchmark as the coldest January in nearly 40 years, a weakening U.S. dollar (USDOLLAR, DXY) and concerns over global oil supply bolstered energy prices. The more defensive utilities (+6.82%), consumer staples (+1.95%) and real estate (+1.50%) sectors benefited from a rise in risk-off sentiment, while the financials (-2.02%) and health care (-3.32%) sectors declined but still outpaced the broader benchmark. The information technology (IT, -9.95%) sector saw the greatest selloff as the unveiling of Chinese AI model DEEPSEEK led to a pullback in AI and data center stocks. The consumer discretionary (-8.24%), industrials (-6.60%), communication services (-4.24%) and materials (-3.46%) also trailed the index.

Against this background the ClearBridge Mid Cap Strategy underperformed its benchmark for the first quarter, as market volatility and declining investor optimism magnified the idiosyncratic headwinds that pressured several of our holdings. Stock selection in the industrials sector was the leading detractor as the prospect of tariffs increasing the cost of manufacturing inputs and weakening economic growth projections weighed on industrial equipment manufacturer Regal Rexnord (RRX) and modular office provider WillScot (WSC). Despite both companies reporting solid fourth-quarter performance, economic uncertainty and the prospect of a further push out in the recovery of short-cycle industrials weighed on their shares.

AI-beneficiary and strong fourth-quarter performer AppLovin (APP) came under pressure from several reports from short sellers seeking to capitalize on a reversal in the company’s strong momentum amid the broader tech retreat. However, we believe the AI-enabled advertising software platform continues to be one of the best AI opportunities within the mid cap market. Recent positive channel indicators for its e-commerce business point toward a solid and persistent future of attractive returns for AppLovin, and we used the short-natured weakness to add to our position.

Likewise, Marvell Technology (MRVL), a networking and storage semiconductor company with a strong presence in data centers, pulled back after a strong run alongside other AI beneficiaries following the DeepSeek announcement. However, we believe that regardless of the impact of less capital and energy intensive AI models like DeepSeek, large AI hyperscalers will continue to build new and more data centers, providing Marvell with a long-term opportunity to capitalize on its position as a preferred partner in their construction.

Our top contributor during the period was EQT, North America’s largest natural gas producer. The company continued its upward trajectory from the fourth quarter as the U.S. endured its coldest winter since 1988, spurring an increase in demand. Additionally, the company continues to capitalize on strong operational performance, making additional progress on its goal of deleveraging and extending its lead as the lowest-cost producer in the basin. The increase in natural gas prices also helped bolster the share price of Baker Hughes (BKR).

Portfolio Positioning

We participated in the IPO of SailPoint (SAIL), which offers identity security solutions to help companies manage and secure access to critical data and applications. Having known the company for a long time and previously owned it in its prior publicly traded forms, we believe the incorporation of AI has swelled SailPoint’s total addressable market — already growing at a high pace — as companies will need to begin addressing what data and functions their new AI agents can access across their enterprise.

Also within the IT sector, we added Microchip Technology (MCHP), which supplies analog and mixed-signal microprocessors for use in the automotive, industrial, computing and other industries. We took advantage of recent weakness in the stock to add to the position. We believe that the return of the company’s prior CEO and efforts to rebuild relationships with customers has resulted in Microchip recovering market share to prior levels, and that it represents an attractive cyclical improvement story with asset optimization improvements and good business visibility.

We exited our position in chemical manufacturer Ashland (ASH). While we were optimistic about the end of destocking trends and a rebound in demand, the company continues to face sluggish end markets without signs of improvement. We redeployed the proceeds to relatively new holding International Paper (IP), which we believe represents a strong turnaround story under the leadership of a new CEO and his plan to optimize its footprint, adjust pricing dynamics and improve margins over the next few years.

Outlook

Visibility and earnings predictability is very low right now. As a result, we are focusing all our attention on companies who control their own destiny and can grow through this malaise. With the threat of more and greater tariffs and economic uncertainty on the horizon, we believe our focus on high-quality stocks with strong balance sheets, pricing power and attractive company-specific drivers is even more important to weathering the economic storm and emerging stronger on the other side. Additionally, we believe the pace and impact of these rapid-fire policy developments highlights the need for a dynamic and actively managed investment process, like ours, which we believe is even more essential to investors during periods of heightened volatility and uncertainty.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Mid Cap Strategy underperformed its Russell Midcap Index during the first quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy had contributions from four of the 11 sectors in which it was invested during the quarter. The largest contributors were the energy and utilities sectors, while the industrials and IT sectors were the greatest detractors.

On a relative basis, stock selection in the industrials, health care, IT, financials and consumer staples sectors weighed on performance.Conversely, stock selection in the communication services and real estate sectors proved beneficial.

On an individual stock basis, the biggest contributors to absolute returns in the quarter were EQT, PPL, Hartford Insurance (HIG), Casey’s General Stores (CASY) and DTE Energy (DTE). The largest detractors from absolute returns were Marvell Technology, Regal Rexnord (RRX), Avantor (AVTR), WillScot (WSC) and AppLovin.

In addition to the transactions listed above, we initiated new positions in Qorvo (QRVO) in the IT sector and Light & Wonder (LNW) in the consumer discretionary sector. We exited positions in Eversource Energy (ES) in the utilities sector, Aspen Technology (ASPN) in the IT sector, Teleflex (TFX) in the health care sector, COTY in the consumer staples sector, KKR in the financials sector and Noble in the energy sector.

Brian Angerame, Managing Director, Portfolio Manager

Matthew Lilling, CFA, Portfolio Manager

data.title: AMD: Should Catch A Bid Soon
data.description:
Introduction

I previously wrote on Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) in February, where I gave AMD a strong buy rating. Since then, the stock has fallen 22%, whilst competitors like NVDA have also fallen ~23% in the same time, signaling a market wide free fall rather than any specific issues with AMD.

In this article, I'll outline why the valuation is getting to a point that is very hard to ignore as a long-term investor, whilst also touching on the reasons to be bullish.

Investment Thesis

A big part of the AMD bull case lies in inference, rather than AI training, which is where NVDA has dominated for the past few years. NVDA no doubt still dominates today with its end to end AI stack with CUDA software, there is going to be a huge amount of demand for a cost-efficient alternative to NVDA's expensive models soon. This is where AMD will slowly, but surely start to win clients from NVDA, though I have no doubt that NVDA will still dominate the vast majority of the market.

My AMD investment thesis does not rely on AMD winning against NVDA. That won't happen. But it does rely on AMD starting to prove they can win in the inference space, which they have already started doing. For example, Oracle (ORCL) has already signed a multi-billion dollar contract with AMD for 30,000 MI355X GPUs.

Essentially, the gold rush in AI is still really only just getting started and there's little chance that all of this can be supplied by just one vendor in NVDA. I've been saying for a while that NVDA's high margin, and complete dominance (almost monopoly) in the space simply isn't sustainable for how vast the market is becoming. The next big players are going to start seeing a lot more opportunities creep up as more and more firms lean into the AI world. If AMD can develop an alternative to NVDA's GPUs which are more cost-effective, and more efficient, the opportunity for them is vast. In the next few months, AMD has the release of their MI350 which will employ chiplet architecture and likely be the big push that AMD needs to start seriously winning share from NVDA.

Risks To Bull Case

The main risk to the bull case above that I see is that:

  1. NVDA will continue to dominate more than I forecast - The pricing power NVDA have over AMD (and the rest of the market) is remarkable (~25bps). As long as NVDA do not become supply constrained, they have complete pricing power in the market, as well as the ability to continue to reinvest in their growth and innovation which they already are. NVDA are investing heavily in industries such as AV's, quantum, robotics etc - the industries of tomorrow. This is all happening whilst AMD is fighting to become the alternative in today's industries.
  2. On top of this, we've also got the dangers of competitors like INTC who have much higher R&D budgets than AMD. AMD has failed to win share in the past few years compared to NVDA simply because they didn't innovate well enough. There's every chance that this continues, and they lose out to the next generation of competitors too.
  3. The final big risk I see is a general macroeconomic risk in tariffs slowing down the global economy and AI CapEx spend. There's also the risk that AMD struggle to onshore their manufacturing at a rate that NVDA can. I don't believe these are specific AMD risks though as much as the first two. Struggles here will cause a slowdown for all semiconductor companies, and not just AMD.

Valuation

AMD is now trading at their lowest earnings multiple in nearly a decade so from a historical basis, this is a clear buy. But I never like to solely look at historical valuations because it doesn't take into account whether the stock was overvalued over the last few years.

Analysts currently estimate $6.2 EPS in FY26, meaning AMD currently trades at 13x FY26 earnings. This means that if AMD only reaches analyst estimates of $6.2 EPS whilst trading at a PE of 20x (same levels as January 2023 PE multiple), then AMD is a $124 stock, which gives a 46% increase from today's price.

For me, a 46% increase is probably not worth the risk of investing in AMD. However, if I look at this a slightly different way in terms of PEG multiples, then I think this valuation paints a slightly different story where the risk to reward is a lot more favorable.

Firstly, AMD currently trades at a PEG of 0.63 which is far below the 1x normal range. If we assume AMD meets analyst estimates of $6.2 in FY26, they will have a 36.8% CAGR over the next 2 years.

At a PEG of 1x, the Price/Earnings multiple is the same as the EPS growth rate so we can assume a PE of 36.8 which would put AMD at $228, or 166% ahead of today's valuation just based on hitting analyst estimates in FY26.

Conclusion

With all this being said, I think the upside potential far outweighs the downside potential for AMD at a PEG of 0.63x and the lowest PE multiple in nearly a decade. With new product launches happening in 2025, increased innovation, and lots of confidence from Lisa Su, the AMD CEO, I think AMD today offers a nice opportunity ahead for investors with a 3-5 year horizon.

data.title: Samsara: Uncertainties Keep Me On The Sidelines
data.description:
Samsara (NYSE:IOT) has fallen significantly since my last publication. Even after the fall I believe that the stock is overvalued because it produces low FCF margins. Additionally, it has high terminal value risk because of the impact of advanced driving automation and ADAS that specifically use cameras for improved driving. Due to the uncertainties, causing a terminal value risk, I rate Samsara a hold.

Background

In my last article about Samsara, I gave a strong opinion about its large P/S combined with low operating and FCF margins. Most high-quality tech companies with high multiples suffered major contractions. Samsara, due to its financial and end market characteristics, was one of the most affected.

Samsara should still be a strong partner for large operation companies that will face lower global growth due to tariffs. Companies will focus more on reducing costs, and Samsara is highly effective in doing so.

Strategy

Streamline, improve and reduce costs on each company's fleet, while creating platformization. Large logistics operators have asset heavy and high labor intensity costs associated with their operations. A slight reduction in the operator cost might translate into a considerable higher profit. Solutions like Samsara’s don’t need to be super advanced and effective to produce small ROI gains. That’s a strong reason for Samsara’s fast growth. Of course, there is competition, and it should only increase with time. To defend its competitive advantage, Samsara is building a comprehensive platform of asset management and optimization with a significant data, feedback and economies of scale advantages.

In the latest earnings call, the company’s management said this:

So some of these newer products, forms is a great example. Asset tags is another one. They're really net new. These are not products that were widely available in the industry. […] That being said, there's competitors for each of these products, but we're really unique in terms of how we structured the whole system as a single platform

Some of the highlights of an IDC study about the benefits of Samsara conclude that the company produces 815% ROI and an average of 2.02 million dollars in fleet benefits per year per company. The fleet benefits include a 6% reduction in overall operating costs, 14% less idle time, 4% savings on fuel, 9% lower maintenance expenses, and a 10% increase in vehicle lifespan.

Samsara complete platform and sky-high ROI should make the company a giant of large-scale operations digitalization and optimization.

Capture a high number of assets under its service and fast. By expanding fast and optimizing for large player deals, IOT is able to absorb more data. Especially data related to safety driving. Whereas route optimization and vehicle preventive maintenance are well-researched and applied in commercial settings, safety driving recommendations and ratings are less researched and there is less data available, opening a large opportunity for Samsara to provide the best safety models. By growing faster, feedback is also more intensive, helping the company implement its system with truck drivers.

There was a growth of 50% in data points and 25% in miles traveled, feeding Samsara safety models, a major differentiation factor for the company.

Financials

Gross margins have consistently been increasing. IOT’s large contract NNR is growing quicker than the small contract one, creating more profitable contracts.

The FCF/EC is high, but most FCF is tied to SBC. There has been a massive increase in this selected metric YoY. Samsara should keep expanding its margins. Consequently, producing higher and higher FCF per unit of employed capital.

There is no, or very little goodwill. All products were built from scratch and are highly effective. Showing a strong R&D company.

The working capital days are negative and are decreasing YoY, which is very positive for the business. There is no working capital invested, which is creating a float that could be invested in other assets, including treasuries.

For now, the analysts are predicting that growth will remain consistent and could even increase in the next three years. Maybe these estimates will change with the increasing impact of tariffs in the global economy. Still, analysts are very positive about Samsara's long-term growth.

Opportunities

Samsara is in the early stages of expansion. A very high number of intense operational businesses are still conducting and managing operations using more simplified approaches and legacy technologies. The ones that wish to surf the wave of increased digitalization should buy from Samsara due to its more advanced and comprehensive operations platform. And it should bring very high results, especially for the larger operations.

In the latest earnings call the company management said this:

First, we're in the early stages of digitizing a massive market. We're generating over $1 billion in ARR from our core vehicle applications alone. Today, less than half of North American commercial vehicles use telematics, and only about 10% use safety products. As our customers prioritize vehicle digitization first, we see a clear path to expanding our core applications into a multi-billion dollar business. More broadly, the market opportunity beyond vehicles is even earlier in digitization. We see an enormous opportunity for sustained growth.

Samsara’s main market is the vehicle digitalization, but its asset tag feature should also enhance its overall portfolio of solutions. However, the product I want to monitor is the safety one. This is the most difficult product to both implement and develop. When it is well implemented it infuses with the day-to-day operations while positively impacting safe driving. The familiarity of the implemented tool and effectiveness should translate to less churn, more differentiation and ROI gains.

We are at the beginning of operations digitalization. Especially in safety driving features that have massive market potential. This market is also very underpenetrated and should be accretive to ARPU, providing a strong growth runaway for revenue and customer lifetime value.

Risks

Now the company sits at a much more attractive P/S, minimizing the risk of multiple compression.

The trend of increased electrification will impact Samsara, because fuel is cheaper. Thus, there should be less opportunities for strong fuel optimization gains, reducing Samsara’s ROI and attractiveness.

Impact of autonomous driving and advanced ADAS is still uncertain on the company.

Valuation

To understand what growth the market is pricing, I like to do a reverse discounted cash flow analysis. To model the discount rate, I use 10%. To be conservative, I assumed an initial FCF margin of 8%. Then it expands 2% every year. To generate the terminal multiple, I’m using a terminal growth rate of 4.7%. The terminal growth rate is obtained summing a real GDP growth rate of 2.5% and an inflation rate (since 2000) of 2.2%, resulting in the number 4.7%. I also take into consideration the share count to calculate the FCF per share. It expands by 3% every year. With the inputs, the growth is balanced to obtain the current share price.

The model outputs an initial growth of 27.8% that reduces 5% every year, reaching 17.5% in 2034. Considering the sell-side analysts' expectations, Samsara is still overvalued. Despite this, Samsara has a high-quality operation, that should be very hard to buy near fair value.

Conclusion

Samsara is a great business. It provides a very strong ROI for its clients, works with some of the largest logistics operators and has a long growth runaway. I like the data-driven advantage they are building. I would like to see its safety product have more penetration, which in theory should translate into significantly superior Customer Lifetime Value. On the negative side, I can’t predict what will happen with the increased development of advanced ADAS and autonomous driving in vehicles. This should impact Samsara business while creating terminal value risks. I rate Samsara a hold.

data.title: Canadian Banks Ranking 2025 - Which Bank To Hold?
data.description:
Let’s rank the Big 6 Canadian banks from worst to best!

Whether you already hold one of these financial giants or are considering adding a bank to your portfolio, this will help you understand key strengths, weaknesses, and long-term investment potential.

Here’s the good, the bad, and the boring.

Scotiabank (BNS)

  • Bear Case: Volatility from international exposure, no dividend increase in 2024 (effectively a passive cut), and the highest payout ratio among peers. Dead last on most key metrics.
  • Mike’s Take: Better long-term opportunities exist. Unless you see a major turnaround, capital might be better placed elsewhere.

CIBC (CM)

  • Bull Case: Strong performance in 2024, solid focus on domestic retail and commercial banking.
  • Bear Case: Limited growth potential, heavy exposure to the Canadian economy, and high concentration of uninsured mortgages.
  • Mike’s Take: Not a bad bank, just others with stronger positioning.

TD Bank (TD)

  • Bull Case: Large U.S. exposure, strong capital position, potential growth from asset recycling.
  • Bear Case: Impact of the money laundering investigation, capped U.S. growth, and some uncertainty around regulatory resolution.
  • Mike’s Take: A deluxe bond with decent yield and modest growth. Good, but overshadowed by stronger peers.

Bank of Montreal (BMO)

  • Bull Case: Aggressive business model, strong U.S. presence, pioneer in private wealth services.
  • Bear Case: Higher volatility and integration risks from acquiring the Bank of the West.
  • Mike’s Take: Upgraded due to TD’s issues. Great option with upside potential.

National Bank (NA:CA)

  • Bull Case: Top long-term performer with strong growth strategy, smart acquisitions (e.g., Canadian Western Bank), solid wealth, and capital markets business.
  • Bear Case: Still the smallest of the big six, concentrated exposure in Quebec, more vulnerable in a localized recession.
  • Mike’s Take: Still a top personal holding. Temporarily behind RBC due to macro uncertainty.

Royal Bank (RY)

  • Bull Case: Huge diversification, balanced revenue mix (banking, wealth, insurance), robust dividend triangle, steady performance.
  • Bear Case: Large mortgage loan book, highly exposed to the Canadian economy.
  • Mike’s Take: Best all-around performer right now. Strong foundation with size, scale, and stability.

Final Thoughts

  • Don’t collect all six banks like Pokémon.
  • Instead, choose your favorite two based on business model and metrics.
  • Diversify across the financial sector: Consider EQ Bank, goeasy (GSY:CA), Propel Holdings (OTCPK:PRLPF)(PRL:CA), TMX Group (OTCPK:TMXXF)(X:CA), Brookfield (BAM), Intact Financial (OTCPK:IFCZF)(IFC:CA), and life insurers like Manulife (MFC)(MFC:CA), Sun Life (SLF)(SLF:CA), and Great-West Life (OTCPK:GWLIF)(GWO:CA).
